pins.name: Telling the Truth So It Moves Someone
pins.description: Pin 5 is the narrative arc in which students turn their observations and evidence into a true story that moves a stranger, learning why a specific detail persuades where a general compliment does not. The demonstration is a drafted profile card tested on a reader, the seed of the final kennel card. The cohort and Guide are the audience for the drafts, with the Eddie and Billie mentor texts as models. It matters because the campaign only changes adoptions if the cards are honest and moving, and that is a craft students must learn, not assume.
pins.objective: Students will write a narrative profile card draft for their assigned dog that shows character through specific observed detail and is revised on reader feedback.
pins.observableEvidence: Each student produces a drafted profile card built from observed detail and a record of the reader feedback that drove a revision.
pins.audience: The Junior cohort and the Guide, with the Eddie and Billie mentor texts as models.
pins.pinNote: yearWeek range 7-8 places the narrative arc in the Presentation phase after the Fluency Gate, feeding the Act 6 showcase in the final week.

subUnits.objective: Students learn what makes a story move a reader, using the Eddie and Billie mentor texts to study how a specific honest detail beats a generic compliment.
subUnits.observableMilestone: Each student picks the strongest true moment about their dog and outlines a profile structured around it.
subUnits.campaignLeanJustification: This sub-unit feeds the Pin 5 draft and the final card by teaching the story-structure and detail-selection craft the card depends on; the skill that crosses forward is choosing the strongest true moment to lead with.
subUnits.targetDokRationale: Students judge which true detail about their dog will move a reader and structure a profile around it, a non-routine craft decision informed by the mentor-text seminars.

sessionTarget.rationale: Concept day 22 carries five lesson lines including two mentor-text seminars (Eddie and Billie); five sessions for a single cohort.

questions.prompt: Why does a specific detail move a reader more than a general compliment?
What are the parts of a story structure?
What is the strongest true moment you observed about your dog?
What made Eddie the Terrible's listing persuasive?
What one detail made Billie Jean go viral?
What is a hook in a story?
questions.expectedAnswer: An opening that pulls the reader in

contentInventory.item: Pick your dog; sort true versus false assumptions from the evidence
Seminar: Eddie the Terrible; honest beats gushing
Seminar: Billie Jean; one true detail

sessions.title: Pick your dog: true versus false
sessions.lessonObjective: Students will be able to sort what people assume about their chosen dog into true and false based on their evidence, and pick the dog to profile.
sessions.description: Students choose the dog they will profile and sort the assumptions about it into true and false using their observation log and investigation evidence. The cognitive work is applying evidence to separate true from false assumptions, mapped reasoning (Bloom: Apply to Analyze; DOK 2-3). The artifact is a true-versus-false assumption sheet for the chosen dog. It opens the narrative arc because the card must lead with what is true, and it honors the Junior care for getting a real animal's story right.

standardsVerbatim.W.NW.6.3: [UNVERIFIED — paraphrase from brief: write narratives to develop real or imagined experiences using effective technique and detail, 2023 NJSLS-ELA Narrative Writing strand; byte-exact text not retrievable]

flags: STANDARD_TAG_UNSURE: W.NW.6.3 strand letters from the brief's migration note; confirm against the NJSLS database.
sessions.observableCheck: Each student's sheet sorts assumptions into true and false with the evidence for each judgment.
sessions.challengeConnection: Feeds Pin 5 by selecting the true material; consumed by session 5.A.2, where the strongest moment is structured into a profile.
anticipatedCognitiveChallenges: Students will keep a flattering assumption the evidence does not support because it makes a better story.
sessions.artifact: The student produces a true-versus-false assumption sheet for their chosen dog, each judgment tied to evidence.

sessions.title: Story structure: hooks and endings
sessions.lessonObjective: Students will be able to structure a dog profile with a hook, a middle that builds, and an ending that lands.
sessions.description: Students learn how a profile is structured with a hook, a building middle, and a landing ending, and pick the strongest true moment to anchor it. The cognitive work is understanding story structure and applying it to their material, a heavy first-teach (Bloom: Understand to Apply; DOK 2-3). The artifact is a profile outline built around the chosen moment. It follows the true-versus-false sort because structure organizes the true material, and it honors the eleven-year-old's growing sense of how to hold an audience.

standardsVerbatim.W.NW.6.3: [UNVERIFIED — paraphrase from brief; byte-exact text not retrievable]

sessions.observableCheck: Each student's outline names a hook, a building middle, and an ending anchored to the strongest true moment.
sessions.challengeConnection: Feeds Pin 5 by structuring the profile; consumed by sessions 5.A.3 and 5.C.1, the writing and drafting.
anticipatedCognitiveChallenges: Students will open with a generic line ('Meet Max!') instead of a hook built from a true moment.
sessions.artifact: The student writes a profile outline with a hook, building middle, and ending anchored to the strongest true moment.

sessions.title: Writing toward the reader
sessions.lessonObjective: Students will be able to write a profile passage aimed at a specific reader, a family walking the adoption kennels.
sessions.description: Students write a profile passage aimed squarely at a family walking the St. Hubert's kennels, choosing words that reach that reader. The cognitive work is applying audience awareness to narrative writing, mapped practice (Bloom: Apply; DOK 2). The artifact is a reader-aimed profile passage. It follows the structure teach to put it into practice, and it honors the Junior pull toward a concrete, named audience.

standardsVerbatim.W.NW.6.3: [UNVERIFIED — paraphrase from brief; byte-exact text not retrievable]

sessions.observableCheck: Each student's passage is aimed at a kennel-walking family, evident in word choice and what it explains.
sessions.challengeConnection: Feeds Pin 5 by drafting reader-aimed prose; consumed by session 5.C.1, the full card draft.
anticipatedCognitiveChallenges: Students will write to a general audience rather than the specific family at the kennel.
sessions.artifact: The student writes a profile passage aimed at a family walking the adoption kennels.

sessions.title: Eddie the Terrible: honest and funny
sessions.tension: Does an honest, unflattering profile move adopters more than a sweet one?

sideA.position: An honest, even unflattering profile moves adopters most because it earns trust, as Eddie the Terrible did.
evidenceSources: the Eddie the Terrible adoption listing (mentor text)
the fact that Eddie was adopted after the honest listing spread

sideB.position: A single sweet true detail moves adopters most because it makes a stranger fall for the dog, as Billie Jean's grin did.
evidenceSources: the Billie Jean viral story (counter-angle text)
the fact that Billie was adopted after one true detail spread

sessions.lessonObjective: Students will be able to explain what makes the Eddie the Terrible listing persuasive, arguing whether honesty or sweetness moves an adopter more.
sessions.description: Students read the Eddie the Terrible listing in seminar and argue whether its honest, unflattering humor is what moves an adopter, setting up the comparison with Billie. The cognitive work is interpreting a mentor text and arguing a contestable position, strategic reasoning in dialogue (Bloom: Analyze to Evaluate; DOK 3). The artifact is each student's seminar position on what made Eddie work, with textual evidence. It opens the honest-versus-sweet tension and shapes the students' own card voice, and it honors the eleven-year-old's delight in honest, funny writing and passionate argument.

standardsVerbatim.W.NW.6.3: [UNVERIFIED — paraphrase from brief; byte-exact text not retrievable]

flags: UNVERIFIED_CONTENT_CLAIM: the Eddie the Terrible listing and its adoption outcome are summarized from the brief; confirm details against the linked HSSV blog post before the seminar.
sessions.observableCheck: Each student states a position on what made Eddie persuasive, citing specific lines from the listing.
sessions.challengeConnection: Feeds Pin 5 and opens the honest-versus-sweet tension; paired with session 5.A.5 and consumed by session 5.C.1, the card draft.
anticipatedCognitiveChallenges: Students will say Eddie works because it is funny without identifying the honesty underneath the humor.
sessions.artifact: The student writes a seminar position on what made the Eddie listing persuasive, citing specific lines.

sessions.title: Billie Jean: one true detail
sessions.tension: Does an honest, unflattering profile move adopters more than a sweet one?

sideA.position: Comedy and full honesty, like Eddie's, move adopters because they disarm and build trust.
evidenceSources: the Eddie the Terrible listing from session 5.A.4

sideB.position: Sincerity and one true detail, like Billie's grin, move adopters because they make a stranger care.
evidenceSources: the Billie Jean viral story (counter-angle text)
the single true detail that spread, her grin when approached

sessions.lessonObjective: Students will be able to compare Billie Jean's one-true-detail sincerity against Eddie's honesty and decide which approach fits their dog.
sessions.description: Students read the Billie Jean story beside Eddie and argue which is more honest and which works, then decide which approach fits their own dog. The cognitive work is comparing two persuasive strategies and choosing one with reasons, strategic evaluative reasoning (Bloom: Evaluate; DOK 3). The artifact is each student's decision on comedy-versus-sincerity for their card, justified from both texts. It closes the honest-versus-sweet tension by turning it into a craft choice, and it honors the thirteen-year-old-adjacent capacity to hold two defensible sides and pick one.

standardsVerbatim.W.NW.6.3: [UNVERIFIED — paraphrase from brief; byte-exact text not retrievable]

flags: UNVERIFIED_CONTENT_CLAIM: the Billie Jean story and its adoption outcome are summarized from the brief; confirm against the linked Yahoo article before the seminar.
sessions.observableCheck: Each student decides comedy or sincerity for their card and justifies it from both mentor texts.
sessions.challengeConnection: Feeds Pin 5 and closes the honest-versus-sweet tension; the chosen approach is consumed by session 5.C.1, the card draft.
anticipatedCognitiveChallenges: Students will pick the approach they like rather than the one that fits their specific dog's true material.
sessions.artifact: The student writes a justified decision on whether comedy or sincerity fits their dog's card, drawing on both mentor texts.

subUnits.objective: Students learn to show rather than tell and to use ad craft and persuasive devices to reveal a dog's character in scenes.
subUnits.observableMilestone: Each student rewrites a told statement about their dog as a shown scene using one persuasive device.
subUnits.campaignLeanJustification: This sub-unit feeds the Pin 5 draft by supplying the show-don't-tell and ad-craft techniques that make the card persuasive; the skill that crosses forward is revealing the dog's character through a scene rather than a label.
subUnits.targetDokRationale: Students apply show-don't-tell and one persuasive device to their dog's material, a familiar multi-step craft move building on the story structure.

sessionTarget.rationale: Concept day 23 carries four lesson lines (show-don't-tell, ad anatomy, target audience and pathos, persuasive devices); three sessions for a single cohort with two lines combined.

questions.prompt: What does show, don't tell mean?
questions.expectedAnswer: Reveal through scene and action, not labels

contentInventory.item: Show, don't tell; reveal character in scenes
Anatomy of an ad; target audience and pathos
Persuasive devices and sequencing the profile

sessions.title: Show, don't tell: scenes over labels
sessions.lessonObjective: Students will be able to rewrite a told statement about their dog as a shown scene that reveals the same character.
sessions.description: Students learn to replace a told label like 'she is friendly' with a shown scene like the dog leaning against a visitor, the move that made the mentor texts work. The cognitive work is applying the show-don't-tell technique, a heavy craft move (Bloom: Apply; DOK 2). The artifact is a told-to-shown rewrite for one trait of the dog. It sits after the mentor-text seminars so students apply what those texts modeled, and it honors the Junior strength in concrete, observed detail.

standardsVerbatim.W.NW.6.3: [UNVERIFIED — paraphrase from brief; byte-exact text not retrievable]

sessions.observableCheck: Each student rewrites one told statement as a shown scene that reveals the same character.
sessions.challengeConnection: Feeds Pin 5 by teaching show-don't-tell; consumed by session 5.C.1, the card draft.
anticipatedCognitiveChallenges: Students will add adjectives to a label rather than replacing it with an observed scene.
sessions.artifact: The student rewrites a told statement about their dog as a shown scene.

sessions.title: Ad anatomy: audience and appeal
sessions.lessonObjective: Students will be able to identify the parts of an ad and choose an emotional appeal suited to their dog and audience.
sessions.description: Students take apart a real ad to see its parts, then choose the emotional appeal that fits their dog and the kennel-walking audience. The cognitive work is analyzing ad structure and applying audience-fit reasoning, mapped practice (Bloom: Analyze to Apply; DOK 2). The artifact is an ad-anatomy breakdown plus a chosen appeal for the dog. It follows show-don't-tell to add persuasive framing, and it honors the Junior interest in how real persuasion is built.

standardsVerbatim.W.NW.6.3: [UNVERIFIED — paraphrase from brief; byte-exact text not retrievable]

sessions.observableCheck: Each student labels the parts of a sample ad and names an emotional appeal suited to their dog and audience.
sessions.challengeConnection: Feeds Pin 5 by framing the appeal; consumed by session 5.C.1, the card draft.
anticipatedCognitiveChallenges: Students will choose an appeal that manipulates rather than one grounded in the dog's true detail.
sessions.artifact: The student produces an ad-anatomy breakdown and a chosen emotional appeal for their dog.

sessions.title: Persuasive devices, sequenced
sessions.lessonObjective: Students will be able to use one persuasive device and sequence the profile so it builds toward the strongest moment.
sessions.description: Students learn a small set of persuasive devices and how sequencing builds a profile toward its strongest moment, the last craft layer before drafting. The cognitive work is understanding persuasive devices and applying sequencing, a heavy craft move (Bloom: Understand to Apply; DOK 2). The artifact is a sequenced profile plan using one named device. It closes the craft sub-unit before the full draft, and it honors the Junior appetite for a clear technique they can deploy.

standardsVerbatim.W.NW.6.3: [UNVERIFIED — paraphrase from brief; byte-exact text not retrievable]

sessions.observableCheck: Each student's profile plan names one persuasive device and sequences toward the strongest moment.
sessions.challengeConnection: Feeds Pin 5 by adding device and sequence; consumed directly by session 5.C.1, the card draft.
anticipatedCognitiveChallenges: Students will front-load the strongest moment, leaving the ending flat.
sessions.artifact: The student writes a sequenced profile plan using one named persuasive device.

subUnits.objective: Students draft their profile card, test it on a reader, and revise from the feedback, producing the Act 5 draft cards.
subUnits.observableMilestone: Each student produces a drafted profile card and a revision driven by specific reader feedback.
subUnits.campaignLeanJustification: This sub-unit is the Pin 5 deliverable and the seed of the final card; the draft-and-revise skill that crosses forward is improving the card against real reader feedback before it goes on a kennel.
subUnits.targetDokRationale: Students draft a card and revise it against reader feedback, a non-routine create-and-revise cycle that produces the campaign artifact.

sessionTarget.rationale: A modeled draft plus a peer-review critique; concept day 24 carries two lesson lines for a single cohort and produces the Act 5 evidence.

questions.prompt: What is the point of peer review?
questions.expectedAnswer: Get feedback to improve the draft

questions.prompt: What is the strongest true moment in your card?

contentInventory.item: Model and draft the profile card (perspective and voice)
Peer review and revise the draft (Act 5 evidence)
contentInventory.notes: Draft profile cards and reader feedback.

sessions.title: Draft the dog card
sessions.lessonObjective: Students will be able to draft a full profile card for their dog, choosing perspective and voice, that shows character through true detail.
sessions.description: Students draft their full profile card, choosing a perspective and voice and assembling structure, shown scenes, and chosen appeal into one piece. The cognitive work is composing the card from all the craft pieces, sustained creation (Bloom: Create; DOK 3). The artifact is each student's first full draft profile card. It is the heart of the Pin 5 deliverable, and it honors the eleven-year-old's pride in a finished, real product with their name on it.

standardsVerbatim.W.WP.6.4: [UNVERIFIED — paraphrase from brief: develop and strengthen writing by planning, revising, and editing, 2023 NJSLS-ELA Writing Process strand; byte-exact text not retrievable]
standardsVerbatim.W.NW.6.3: [UNVERIFIED — paraphrase from brief; byte-exact text not retrievable]

flags: STANDARD_TAG_UNSURE: W.WP.6.4 strand letters from the brief's migration note; confirm against the NJSLS database.
sessions.observableCheck: Each student's draft card shows character through at least two true observed details and follows their sequence plan.
sessions.challengeConnection: Feeds Pin 5 by producing the draft; consumed by session 5.C.2, the peer review, and by the Act 6 finalization.
anticipatedCognitiveChallenges: Students will revert to generic praise under the pressure of a full draft, dropping the shown detail.
sessions.artifact: The student drafts a full profile card showing character through true observed detail.

sessions.title: Peer review: revise from a reader
sessions.lessonObjective: Students will be able to give kind, specific, helpful feedback on a peer's card and revise their own from the feedback they receive.
sessions.description: Students test their draft on a peer reader, give kind, specific, helpful feedback, and revise their own card from what they hear. The cognitive work is evaluating a draft against reader response and revising, evaluative reasoning (Bloom: Evaluate; DOK 3). The artifact is the revised card plus a note of the feedback that drove the change, the Act 5 evidence. It closes the narrative arc and feeds the final card, and it honors the eleven-year-old's resistance to revision by framing it as responding to a real reader, with short rounds and a kind norm.

standardsVerbatim.W.WP.6.4: [UNVERIFIED — paraphrase from brief; byte-exact text not retrievable]

sessions.observableCheck: Each student gives kind, specific, helpful feedback on a peer card and records one revision their own feedback drove.
sessions.challengeConnection: Is the Pin 5 deliverable; the revised draft is consumed by session 6.A.1, where the card is finalized.
sessions.developmentalNote: Re-norm kind, specific, helpful at the start; keep revision rounds short for eleven-year-old resistance to revising prior work.
anticipatedCognitiveChallenges: Students will give only praise in peer review, leaving the writer nothing to revise.
sessions.artifact: The student produces a revised profile card with a note of the reader feedback that drove the revision.
